narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict bar exam success. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.30, an LSAT score of 26, and plans to attend full-time. The model predicts a 92% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, as it stood out sharply compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance. Your LSAT score was the next strongest influence in the same direction, well below the average of around 37 that most admitted students achieve. Your first-year decile at the bottom also contributed noticeably toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward passing, performing below average but helping offset some risks. Your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans provided small boosts toward success as well, aligning with common profiles of passers. Compared to typical students, your grades and LSAT are in the lower tail of the distribution, where failure rates are much higher, while your other factors are more average. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person—many overcome similar starts with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying once you've addressed these key areas. We're rooting for your success, and clearer signals of bar exam readiness could open doors next time.
